name: RawData+opencog-ml+OpenCog dialog
content:
Source: opencog-dev mailing list
Thread: OpenCog dialog
Date range: Wed, 13 Apr 2016 21:28:55 -0500 to Wed, 27 Apr 2016 10:30:04 -0700
Messages: 13
Participants: Linas Vepstas, Ben Goertzel, Ed Pell, Eddie Monroe
---

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Wed, 13 Apr 2016 21:28:55 -0500

Ben, below is a draft description of the current code base. I am hoping
that it will clarify what last night's argument was about.

If you are impatient, jump to the section titled "The existing code base
(March/April 2016)"  This lists the "five steps" I was talking about.   The
OpenPsi proposal handles maybe one or two of these steps, but not all
five.  That is why I argued.

After that, jump to the section "Lessons learned: Subsystem we don't have,
but we need"   After prototyping, it became painfully clear what it is that
we are missing.  These are huge holes in the plan, and all I have is a
rough prototype.

The section called "Overall design philosophy" describes what I plan to
work on next.

The section "Appendix A" is some of the stuff that we really need to have,
and can be [...]

---

From: Ben Goertzel
Date: Thu, 14 Apr 2016 11:05:32 +0800

Thanks Linas!

Today is overbusy with  meetings, but I have a 16 hr flight starting
at 1AM, and I will read this on the flight and then respond in detail
sometime during the weekend or at worst on Monday....  We will fit all
the pieces together ;)

From: Ben Goertzel
Date: Tue, 19 Apr 2016 23:47:54 -0400

BTW I have chosen not to respond to this thread till I have time to
write a careful response, which it seems like will occur on the
weekend...

Regarding OpenPsi, I believe that since writing that message to which
you're understanding, Linas has come to more fully understand what it
does and what it isn't claimed to do....  Indeed it only handles a
small part of the picture, but I continue to think it handles that
part just fine..

I note that since that message you're responding to was sent, a
first-pass port of Linas's robot behavior script to "OpenPsi format"
has been coded already (by Mandeep and Misgana); and Amen and Man Hin
have made various tweaks to OpenPsi to make sure it works properly...

-- Ben

---

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Wed, 20 Apr 2016 11:11:14 -0500

Hi Ed,

Please interpret my silence as agreement.

--linas

---

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Wed, 20 Apr 2016 11:57:08 -0500

Hi Ean,

Heh. I spend very little of my time thinking. It is very easy to let theory
outstrip reality; I prefer to implement, and see what happens, and use that
as a guide to theory.  So mostly I implement, and that is a fairly
mindless, thankless task.

The goal here was to describe (to Ben, and others) what actually happened
when I went to implement stuff.  It revealed areas where the current system
is weak, and where sufficient architecture is still lacking.

Eh? hardly. Dekang Lin did work on this in the 1990's.  Domingos has whizzy
ports of these ideas to markov logic nets, done in the mid-2000's.  There's
entire research journals and annual conventions devoted to this stuff.
There are mailing lists on which arguments break out.  I got into one
argument that was later published as [...]

---

From: Ben Goertzel
Date: Wed, 20 Apr 2016 13:26:41 -0400

I agree with linas ... more later

---

From: Ben Goertzel
Date: Thu, 21 Apr 2016 04:28:23 -0400

Well, not to put to fine a point on it, but... perhaps that's because
you have not been thinking about the problem intensively for a long
period of time, and have not studied AI and cognitive science
comprehensively ;p

OpenPsi is an intentionally simple framework and not intended to
capture all the interior processes that go into influencing mood and
making decisions.  I am not sure where or why this misconception crept
into the discussion...   It is intended only as a small part of a
larger framework for doing this (and the two cognitive architctures
that use Psi, i.e. Micropsi and Opsncog, both use it together with a
variety of other mechanisms)

-- ben

---

From: Ben Goertzel
Date: Thu, 21 Apr 2016 04:41:52 -0400

Linas, etc.

Finally I find time to reply...

I feel bad distracting you from technical work with a long email --
but your long email from last week deserved an in-depth response, so
here goes...

*****
The general strategy is this:
0. Raw sensory input.
1. Identify what is being asked (said, perceived)
2. Assemble a set of candidate answers (responses)
3. Seek evidence for or against each answer (each action to be taken)
4. Formulate a reply (perform that action)
5. Raw motor and animation.
*****

This is obviously a simplified pipeline, but it's reasonable for a start.

One assumption made here is that composite perception is not happening, that
even complex perception (e.g. face identification) is being treated as
"raw data"
even though actually it's the result of complex processing.  [...]

Relex2Frame used hand-crafted rules, [...]

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Mon, 25 Apr 2016 22:14:28 -0500

Hi Ben,

Don't -- some of these issues are important, and failure to talk leads tto
miscommunication later on. Best to be "on the same page".

Yes, agreed.  But I can always invert the process:  No matter how much
integrated processing is done *inside* of the atomspace, there is always,
defacto, something happening outside of it.

The practical question is then where to draw this line between "inside" and
"outside", as of this year, vs. next year.  Leaving all of vision, and most
of audition outside of opencog is just fine by me, for the next year or
three.

Lack of integrated vision or audition does not seem to be a stumbling block
or a bottleneck for any short term plans.   However, having a much broader
and richer set of sensory inputs is very clearly a bottleneck.  Opencog is
very [...]

---

From: Ben Goertzel
Date: Wed, 27 Apr 2016 10:30:04 -0700

Hi Linas,

Again I find myself too busy with money-grubbing and
organizational/planning activities to spend a lot of time on email...
maybe I'll come back to this email later, or we'll discuss F2F in HK
soon ...

I agree totally with your key point that what we want are "bite sized
pieces", e.g. ImplicationLinks covering small snippets of behavior and
context, rather than ImplicationLinks encompassing big inclusive rules
...  The big inclusive rules in my email were just intended as
examples to illustrate the structures and mechanisms I thought were
appropriate...

Well, if they are not doing what you need them to do, in general my
hope is that you would fix/improve them or make specific suggestions
to others (Nil for the URE, Amen and Man Hin for OpenPsi) regarding
how to fix/improve [...]
